# Preparing a lab report





## Using R Markdown

In RStudio, open an R Markdown document by selecting File \> New File \> R Markdown.
When the dialog box opens, you can add a preliminary name to the file (but this is not yet required until you save the file), so you can simply click 'OK'.
At the top of the R Markdown document, enter the following code (include the three dashes at the top and bottom):

```         
---
title: "Lab No. #"
author: 
  - name: "Your Name"
    affiliation: "DATA 202, Fall 2024, Howard University"
date: "2024-09-07"
always_allow_html: true
output: 
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    number_sections: true
    self_contained: yes
    mode: selfcontained
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    number_sections: true
geometry: margin=1.0in
---
```

This is an R Markdown document.
Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents.
For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document.
You can embed an R code chunk like this:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
summary(cars)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
     speed           dist       
 Min.   : 4.0   Min.   :  2.00  
 1st Qu.:12.0   1st Qu.: 26.00  
 Median :15.0   Median : 36.00  
 Mean   :15.4   Mean   : 42.98  
 3rd Qu.:19.0   3rd Qu.: 56.00  
 Max.   :25.0   Max.   :120.00  
```
:::
:::


## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](lab-template_files/figure-html/pressure-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.


::: {.cell}

```{.python .cell-code}
x = [1, 2, 3]
```
:::

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
py$x
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
[1] 1 2 3
```
:::
:::

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
py$y <- head(cars)
```
:::

::: {.cell}

```{.python .cell-code}
print(y)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
   speed  dist
0    4.0   2.0
1    4.0  10.0
2    7.0   4.0
3    7.0  22.0
4    8.0  16.0
5    9.0  10.0
```
:::
:::
